So from our end, we don't communicate to your clients. So at one moment, there won't be any access to the resources anymore. That's what currently happens. So I'll give you an example. When we are, if you like, under reciprocation, right, gifts, what we talked about is intangibles. Make it clear when you do something for people. And I'm as guilty as the next, oh, don't worry about it. It's not a problem I do for anybody. And you lose that opportunity to say, hey, no, I actually put in some serious effort for you, because it's important for me to ensure that blah, blah, blah, and one of the things that comes out of the way that we train people is ideally you would say something along the lines is I'm sure you will do the same thing for me if I'm in the same situation sometime in the future. Won't you pause and get them to react? So you lock in that commitment. Yeah, yeah, yeah, you optimize even our training. Yeah, great. Now, of course, I get a lot of pushback, certainly from Britt saying, whoa, that's way too confrontational. Yeah. And my explanation, and I always explain is also when I do a teaching, is now you're trying to act like the rational human being that we all want to believe we are when it comes to decision making. And I move back to system one system two, I say now you see the clear difference. Now you are fully analyzing this example, and then it's suddenly to American, we have examples from all over the world that this works, we now only used an American example where it was applied. But now you are analyzing what happens in system two, you don't think it would work on you because you are coming from a very rational point of view now. Well, actually in the moment of decision making, the likelihood that you won't come from that rational point of view, but as Daniel Kahneman framed it, your system one point of view driven by shortcuts that form the decision triggers equals the principles of persuasion. And then your Kahneman has a saying, there's no nothing more important than the thing you're thinking about. So when you raise subtle things to awareness, it's top of mind and suddenly it's super important apparently in our thinking and therefore our decision making. I am wonderfully well. Thanks, Ily. Great to see everybody. Some familiar faces around the table. Fantastic. Listen, I've been, I've done a couple of sessions persuasion influencing now with, so my niche is procurement and purchasing people, right? So these guys think they're really hard-nosed, really rational. The stakeholders they're dealing with are also entirely rational. They're a little bit skeptical about the power of System One and these decision triggers. Now, I know Bas in the past when we've talked about this, you mentioned, yeah, but they're human beings too, which is correct, of course. But is there any kind of any other tangible example that you have where someone like from a really hard-nosed, an area of business known for making System Two decisions? Is there any, any example you can give that can help me sway the audience to really understand the power of what we're teaching in this specific field? Yes. I come up right away with one. I can think of maybe a few more. I don't come up right away, my apologies for that, Mart, with procurement. But I come up with a CEO from a Fortune-Hunders company. And that's always when people say, no, I don't act like that. So you don't do it. So how do you think a CEO of a Fortune-Hunders company would act rational, right? And I think the night of negotiation study is even in the practitioner course. I can briefly explain it. I know basically almost for sure, but it was when doctors, they, they grew two ways, organically and by merchant acquisition. They just were buying other companies. And of course, a Fortune-Hunders company doesn't buy the local bakery. They buy other huge companies. So they start to negotiate with the other CEO. One story short, Dr. Knight was called in by the strategic director because he thought these processes could be shorter. What they, what Dr. Knight had found was the CEO was always five minutes late in the meeting, phone still on his ear. And it was time, time is money and right away to business. So two things was asked to a CEO here from a Fortune-Hunders company to change. One, if the meeting started at 10 a.m., you are there 9.55 and no phones on your ear anymore. You are there. It's a liking principle, right? The second thing was, Dr. Knight had got a team and they studied what does this, what is the CEO, where he grew up, what school he has, what is hobby. So they had a list and then they compared those to the CEO's he was going to speak to. And they found similarities and the CEO was instructed to talk for at least 15 minutes to the other CEO before they came to business, before they started talking about business, about those topics. He first refused. He didn't want to do it to CEO. They have no time, time is precious, 15 minutes, that's way too long. First meeting, Dr. Knight could persuade him, of course. First meeting, he was talking for 45 minutes about sailing and they already planned, well, we should start sailing with our families. But one story short, this shortened the entire process of the merchant acquisition on average by three months, from nine months to six months. On average, was there somewhere between 15 and 30 million dollars saving per acquisition, which they measured over 10 acquisitions, which was over 200 million dollars saving. Why? It was built a human connection before these two people were starting how one CEO was buying the company from the other CEO. I don't believe people see more ration, what they think are more rational creatures than CEO's of 1400 companies. Yeah, that's a great example. Yeah, would that help? Yeah, I'll try it. I hope. Otherwise, I have to dig somewhere because I definitely procurement. And I also came up right away, it's a personal example, which everybody faces, but it doesn't pop up right away, that something that everybody experienced. But we can definitely work on that. Everybody makes similar irrational choices in their life. Yeah, marvelous. Yeah, if you get the chance to dig something up, then that would be really appreciated. Thank you. Yeah, great. Ross, would you mind if I share a quick story? Yeah, no, of course, please. So my wife many years ago had a severe pain in the back of her head, and then she had it again within 24 hours. So on the advice of a friend, we went to the emergency room. We're meeting with the emergency room doctor, and he asks what's going on, she describes it. And then she said, and I was scared because my brother had brain surgery last year because of an aneurysm. And so of course, that really concerned the doctor. Well, he had to step out of the room. So we made a phone call to our sister-in-law and asked her, I said, what happened with Carl? And she said, and Jane asked, was it an aneurysm? She said, no, he fell, hit his head, he had brain swelling. And as a result, they had to do surgery to relieve the tension on his brain. Doctor comes back in and we tell him it wasn't an aneurysm. It was a sub dermal hematoma, brain swelling. And he lowered his head and he shook it and he said, I still can't get the aneurysm out of my mind. And I think we should do these tests. So I asked him, I said, so let me be clear here, if we had never mentioned aneurysm, you wouldn't be suggesting the tests. And he said, that's correct. But I can't get it out of my mind. And so therefore, I think we should have the test. Now, I wasn't going to argue with my wife because she was concerned, but it showed that even the most rational people, I think doctors are even more rational than CEOs. And he knew that it was irrelevant information, but based on feeling he couldn't let it go. Love that story, Brian. Great, thanks, Brian. And I know there's actually research, because I dig that up on time, because I have a big pharma client that every doctor, sorry, not every, the far majority of medical specialists believe they are not receptive to these principles because they think they are so rational. That was there was research done. And of course, they were just as receptive to these principles as every other human being. So well, the second great one, Brian, thanks for adding that. Very good.
